itineraries.source_url: https://www.canterbury.co.uk/whitstable-harbour/, https://www.thekenttourist.co.uk/articles/whitstable-harbour-guide, https://www.english-heritage.org.uk/visit/places/deal-castle/, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Deal_Castle, https://www.thekenttourist.co.uk/creative-quarter-folkestone, https://www.mytenterden.co.uk/directory/tenterden-history-and-heritage-article-96.aspx, https://cinqueports.org/tenterden/, https://www.nationaltrust.org.uk/visit/kent/sissinghurst-castle-garden, https://www.kentgardenstrust.org.uk/garden/sissinghurst-castle-near-cranbrook/, https://www.historyhit.com/locations/medieval-rye/, https://ryemuseum.co.uk/, https://www.britainexpress.com/counties/eastsussex/az/winchelsea.htm, https://cinqueports.org/winchelsea/, https://www.nationaltrust.org.uk/visit/sussex/bodiam-castle, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bodiam_Castle, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hastings_Old_Town, https://www.english-heritage.org.uk/visit/places/1066-battle-of-hastings-abbey-and-battlefield/, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Battle_Abbey, https://www.nationaltrust.org.uk/visit/sussex/batemans, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bateman%27s, https://www.greatbritishlife.co.uk/magazines/sussex/22574858.closer-look-history-mayfield/, https://poohcorner.co.uk/ashdown-forest/, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Forest_Row, https://www.ditchlingmuseum.com, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ditchling, https://www.britainexpress.com/attractions.htm?attraction=3138, https://www.rspb.org.uk/days-out/reserves/pulborough-brooks, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Amberley_Museum_%26_Heritage_Centre, https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cowdray_House, https://www.cowdray.co.uk/historic-cowdray/
itineraries.title: Kent and Sussex in 12 Days: The Weald, Coast and Cinque Ports
itineraries.subtitle: From Whitstable to the Midhurst ruins, a sweeping arc through the medieval Weald and the Cinque Ports.
itineraries.notes: Route designed for the warmer months (May–September); some sites like Sissinghurst gardens and Bodiam Castle require advance booking. Weald roads are narrow: allow extra time between stops.

visits.text: The scent of sun-warmed oysters on the harbour stalls reaches you before you see the quay. This working port has been here since 1832, built for one of England's first passenger railway lines—coal first, tourists later. Oyster cultivation here is documented back to Roman times. Avoid July–August: queues at the stalls triple.

Harbour Street strikes a balance between kitsch and charm: independent shops and galleries occupy colourful timber buildings. This is no ordinary souvenir strip—butchers and greengrocers still hold their ground against the artisans. The atmosphere remains intact. Arrive early in the morning to see it before the tour groups.

Starvation Point, a discreet memorial at the end of the promenade, recalls two stories at once: sailors lost at sea and the casual labourers who queued at the port with no guarantee of work. Two lines of text on a single plinth. More effective than ten panels.

activities.text: Order a dozen native oysters from the harbour stalls and eat them standing, facing the sea: this is how it's been done for centuries. Whitstable's flat oysters have a reputation that goes back to Roman Britain. Expect around £1.50 per oyster depending on the season.

practical_info.tip: Avoid sit-down restaurants on Friday evenings in summer: very busy. Stick to the market stalls.

activities.text: If the tide permits, the sailing barge Greta offers trips out to the Maunsell Forts at sea—WWII metal structures planted a few miles offshore. An absolute anachronism off a shingle beach.

practical_info.tip: Book the Greta in advance via Whitstable Harbour. The forts are only accessible by boat.

visits.text: Beneath its flattened wedding-cake profile, Deal Castle conceals more than 140 positions designed for cannons. Henry VIII had it built between 1539 and 1540 in response to the alliance between Francis I and Charles V. In plan, it resembles a Tudor rose: a circular keep surrounded by six semicircular bastions. Anne of Cleves stopped here in December 1539 before meeting the king. Entry fee applies; free for English Heritage members.

Locals simply call it 'the prom': Deal's seafront promenade runs along the Channel for several hundred metres, lined with Georgian houses whose façades face the waves. Deal Pier—Kent's last remaining pleasure pier—juts out into the sea. People have fished from its railings since the structure went up in the 1950s.

activities.text: Walk south along the High Street to Walmer Castle, about twenty minutes on foot: official residence of the Lord Warden of the Cinque Ports since the eighteenth century. Wellington died here in 1852 in his campaign chair—the chair is still there, in his bedroom.

practical_info.tip: Walmer Castle is managed by English Heritage. Closed in winter except weekends. Check opening times before setting out.

visits.text: Folkestone's Creative Quarter is a genuine success story of urban regeneration. Since 2002, Creative Folkestone has restored the Victorian façades of the Old High Street and Tontine Street into studios and galleries. Look for murals from the Folkestone Triennial—new installations every three years.

The smell of fish and chips, fresh paint and salt air mingles at the Harbour Arm: this revamped Victorian pier gathers food trucks, bars and an open-air gallery. An Antony Gormley cast-iron figure from the Another Time series stands in the tidal zone on the old loading platform.

The Leas Promenade, Victorian and genuinely handsome, follows the clifftop for several kilometres. Direct views across the Channel, with the French coast visible on clear days. The Leas Cliff Hall dates from the 1920s. The Leas Lift funicular, built in 1885, still links the upper promenade to the lower beach—ride it rather than walk.

activities.text: Head down to the Harbour Arm in the evening for the bars and impromptu gigs. The train from London St Pancras takes 55 minutes—many Londoners do the round trip in a day just for this quarter. Friday and Saturday nights, the atmosphere builds quickly.

practical_info.tip: Access to the Harbour Arm is free. Events and concerts sometimes have an entry fee. Check creativefolkestone.org.uk.

activities.text: If you have an extra day from Folkestone, Dungeness is worth the detour (25 km): Prospect Cottage, filmmaker Derek Jarman's former house, shingle garden facing the nuclear power station—garden visible from the road, no interior access.

practical_info.tip: No tourist facilities at Dungeness. Bring water and snacks. Direct route from Folkestone via Lydd.

visits.text: A port without the sea since the fifteenth century, Tenterden still appears in the roster of the Cinque Ports. In 1449 the town was attached as a limb of Rye. Coastal change then cut off all access to the Channel: the town now sits sixteen kilometres inland. The High Street and its Georgian façades bear witness to wealth built on wool and ships.

Attached to the Cinque Ports in 1449 as a limb of Rye, Tenterden enjoyed tax exemption and representation at royal coronations—a privilege still exercised at the coronation of Charles III. The pinnacled tower of St Mildred's Church once served as a beacon against the Spanish Armada. Nelson's daughter, Horatia, was the vicar's wife.

activities.text: Board the Kent & East Sussex Railway from Tenterden Town station: steam locomotives, period rolling stock, Weald scenery. The line connects Tenterden to Bodiam—a stop to combine with tomorrow's castle visit. Check timetables at kesr.org.uk; some services are seasonal.

practical_info.tip: Some combined National Trust tickets include the Tenterden–Bodiam return. 10% discount if you've visited Bodiam Castle.

visits.text: The sails of Union Mill turn slowly above Cranbrook—the tallest white smock mill in England, built in 1814 and still in working order. It grinds grain on certain weekends. Climb inside to see the timber gears at work. Open Saturdays and some bank holidays.

Cranbrook's High Street, genuinely preserved by its distance from major routes: fifteenth- and sixteenth-century timber-framed façades, weatherboarded houses. The town grew rich on wool in the Middle Ages, then on the Flemish weavers' guild. St Dunstan's Church dates from the fifteenth century and dominates the skyline.

activities.text: Look for the Cranbrook Museum to understand the town's role in the Weald's wool industry—collections of textiles and local materials. Small community museum open afternoons. Afterwards, a short walk into the surrounding woods is enough to sense why Kipling loved this region.

practical_info.tip: Cranbrook Museum is open Tuesday–Saturday afternoons. Free entry. Check seasonal hours.

visits.text: Vita Sackville-West's White Garden: an obsession that became a global reference. Conceived in the 1930s with her husband Harold Nicolson, this succession of 'outdoor rooms' across nine acres runs the spectrum from white roses to silver herbs. Each compartment has its own logic. Online booking essential in high season.

The Elizabethan red-brick tower dates from the 1560s—almost all that remains of the Bakers' great manor house, ruined after the Civil War. Vita Sackville-West installed her study here and wrote her novels and poems. The view from the top of the tower over the gardens and the Weald is the best on the estate.

activities.text: Climb the tower before 10 a.m.: no one else will be there and the morning light on the garden below is particular. Beyond that, the 450-acre estate surrounding the formal gardens can be walked—meadow, ancient woods, farmland. Allow at least two hours.

practical_info.tip: Garden entry: non-members around £16–18. Parking £4 for non-members. GPS sometimes leads to the wrong location—follow the National Trust signs.

visits.text: The cobbles of Mermaid Street glisten after rain: this is the most photographed street in Sussex, and it's easy to see why. The Mermaid Inn halfway up the street has had cellars here since 1156—the current building dates to 1420, rebuilt after a French raid. The Hawkhurst Gang, an eighteenth-century smuggling band, used it as a hideout. Walk up from the bottom for the best view.

Ypres Tower, built in the thirteenth century, served as prison, courthouse and warehouse before becoming the Rye Castle Museum. Its name comes from a fifteenth-century owner, John de Ypres. The interior displays medieval armour, town models and a section dedicated to smuggling. Open daily.

Rye folk call their town an island—the etymology confirms it: 'rye' derives from the Old Saxon 'ieg', island. On medieval maps, Rye was surrounded by water. Today St Mary's Church has crowned the hilltop for more than nine hundred years. Climb the tower: on clear days, the French coast is visible.

activities.text: Climb the tower of St Mary's Church for a 360° view over Rye's rooftops, the marshes and the sea. On the way down, look for the Quarter Boys: the two automata on the external clock face that strike the quarters rather than the hours—a thoroughly local medieval quirk.

practical_info.tip: Access to the tower is paid (a few pounds). No booking needed. Avoid lunch hour: queues.

activities.text: If the weather's good, Rye Harbour Nature Reserve spreads out twenty minutes' walk to the south: lagoons, reedbeds and one of England's largest colonies of common terns in summer. Free entry. Bring binoculars.

practical_info.tip: The reserve is open all year. Terns nest May–July. Waterproof footwear recommended on muddy paths.

visits.text: A planned town by Edward I in 1281, razed and rebuilt on a hill after a devastating 1287 storm engulfed the old town—now beneath the waves. Winchelsea is one of the best-preserved examples of medieval town planning in England: grid streets, more than 140 listed buildings. Today perhaps England's smallest town, with only a few hundred inhabitants.

St Thomas the Martyr Church, truncated by fourteenth-century French raids, was never fully rebuilt—the nave is still missing. What remains is sublime: six medieval canopied tombs including that of Admiral Gervase Alard (1312), the oldest admiral effigy in England. Spike Milligan is buried in the churchyard.

activities.text: Descend into Winchelsea's medieval cellars—an underground network of stone vaults testifying to the town's role in the wine trade in the fourteenth century, when it was one of the kingdom's principal ports. The Court Hall Museum organises guided tours by appointment in high season.

practical_info.tip: The Court Hall Museum opens in spring and summer, run by volunteers. Confirm hours at cinqueports.org before visiting.

visits.text: Bodiam Castle reflected in its moat: too perfect, you'd think it was a set. The Monty Python and the Holy Grail crew certainly thought so, using it briefly for an exterior shot of Swamp Castle. Built in 1385 by Sir Edward Dalyngrigge, knight of Edward III, to defend the Rother valley against the French during the Hundred Years' War. The exterior is almost intact; the interior is a roofless ruin open to the wind.

Built in 1385 by Sir Edward Dalyngrigge, knight of Edward III, Bodiam is a courtyard castle: four round corner towers, two gates framed by rectangular towers. The keyhole-shaped arrow slits bear witness to the use of early portable firearms. The moat was drained during excavations in 1970.

activities.text: Take the Kent & East Sussex Railway from Tenterden if you missed it yesterday—the line stops a stone's throw from the castle. Otherwise, the walk from the car park follows the Rother bank for half an hour. The approach on foot is far better: the castle appears suddenly above the reeds.

practical_info.tip: Parking for non-members: £5. Free entry for National Trust members. No booking required. Café on site.

visits.text: The black fishermen's sheds at The Stade, cracked tar and century-old planks, stand in rows on the shingle beach: unique in Europe. These Net Shops—around 39 still standing—were built tall for lack of space on the beach, after a new shingle bank appeared in 1834. Hastings houses the largest beach-launched fishing fleet in Britain.

Hastings Old Town, squeezed between East Hill and West Hill, is a succession of narrow lanes called 'twittens', Tudor timber-framed houses and Georgian façades. The High Street leads to the antiques market and independent galleries. Nothing polished for tourists: this is still a neighbourhood that lives.

Hastings Contemporary, an ultra-modern gallery planted right next to the black timber fishermen's sheds: the contrast is deliberate. Opened in 2012, it presents a collection of British contemporary art and temporary exhibitions. Adult entry around £8—free on the first Tuesday of each month between 4 p.m. and 8 p.m.

activities.text: Take the East Hill Cliff Railway funicular from Rock-a-Nore Road: opened in 1902, it's the steepest in the UK according to some local sources. It leads to the top of East Hill and Hastings Country Park, with views over the Channel and the Weald. Come down the steps for variety.

practical_info.tip: The funicular has restricted hours off-season. Check before going: the steps are the alternative but steep.

activities.text: If the tide allows, buy fish directly from the fishermen or stalls on Rock-a-Nore Road early in the morning. Maggie's Fish and Chips on site is a safe bet. Some boats return before 8 a.m.—watch from the beach.

practical_info.tip: Fresh fish stalls open mornings only. Arrive before 9 a.m. for the best choice.

map_keywords: Battle Abbey East Sussex English Heritage

visits.text: On 14 October 1066, on this bare hilltop, two armies met and England changed language. William built Battle Abbey in penance for the slaughter—the high altar was placed at the exact spot where Harold died. A slab still marks the position in the grass. The free audio guide walks the battlefield with wooden markers.

Battle's High Street, medieval façades too quiet for a town that gave its name to a decisive battle. Walking up towards the abbey, sixteenth-century timber-framed fronts follow the shops. Battle also became, in the seventeenth century, England's main centre of gunpowder production—supplying the army until the Crimean War.

activities.text: Follow the audio guide on the Battle Abbey battlefield: it reconstructs the Norman advance, the Saxon resistance and Harold's death. The visitor centre's interactive panels include CGI reconstructions. During the annual October re-enactment weekend, more than 500 participants recreate the fighting on the original site.

practical_info.tip: Battle Abbey is managed by English Heritage. Open daily 10 a.m.–5 p.m. (4 p.m. in winter). Discount for members. Annual re-enactment in October: check dates at english-heritage.org.uk.

visits.text: Kipling wrote when he first saw the house: 'That's She! The Only She! Make an honest woman of her quick!' Bateman's, a Jacobean house from 1634 in Burwash, was his residence from 1902 until his death in 1936. He wrote Puck of Pook's Hill and the poem 'If' here. The dining room with its eighteenth-century silver-leather hangings is the finest room in the house.

The garden at Bateman's, clipped box and mill wheels by the Dudwell: Kipling felt like an English country gentleman here. The thirteenth-century water mill on the property inspired his Puck stories. You can walk to the ponds through the 300-acre estate.

activities.text: Walk around Burwash village before leaving: a bronze statue of Kipling unveiled in 2018 stands in the village centre, piercing gaze. The village preserves seventeenth-century timber-framed façades and a pub, The Bear Inn, that has changed little since the writer's time.

practical_info.tip: Bateman's National Trust: entry around £12. Open Friday–Tuesday. Mulberry Café on site. Parking free for members, otherwise £4 per car.

map_keywords: Mayfield Palace Old School East Sussex

visits.text: An archbishop's palace now converted into a Catholic school—such is the fate of Mayfield Palace, medieval residence of the Archbishops of Canterbury since St Dunstan in the tenth century. The fifteenth-century dormitory and fourteenth-century great hall (now the school chapel) are among the finest medieval halls in Sussex. The property is private but visible from the road.

Mayfield's High Street counts 40 officially listed buildings on a single street—a rare concentration even in the Weald. Most date from the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, financed by the profits of the local iron industry. Middle House, built in the second half of the sixteenth century, is now a pub with a carved fireplace.

activities.text: Look for the legend of St Dunstan and the Devil: the archbishop was working as a blacksmith when the Devil, disguised as a beautiful woman, tried to seduce him. Dunstan spotted the cloven hoof and pinched Satan's nose with red-hot tongs. The Devil fled to soothe his burn at the springs of Tunbridge Wells. The tongs are reportedly still kept at Mayfield School.

practical_info.tip: The village can be walked in 30 minutes. Good lunch stop at Middle House before continuing to Hartfield.

visits.text: Poohsticks Bridge: ridiculously charming at 40 as it is at six. This wooden bridge in the middle of Ashdown Forest is officially called Posingford Bridge—built in 1907. A. A. Milne moved to Cotchford Farm in Hartfield in 1925 and invented the Hundred Acre Wood. Pooh Corner in the village serves bear-shaped sandwiches.

The bridge dates from 1907; A. A. Milne arrived in 1925: Ashdown Forest has changed little since. The Enchanted Place is at the top of the forest, on a heather-covered heath with panoramic views—a plaque dedicated to Milne and illustrator E. H. Shepard marks the spot. This is where Christopher Robin says goodbye to Winnie the Pooh.

activities.text: Walk through Ashdown Forest to the Enchanted Place: the hike from Pooh Corner is around 4.7 km return, easy. Look along the way for Owl's House and Piglet's House—marked by discreet wooden signs. Forest car parks are now paid (around £1.50 per week or per day).

practical_info.tip: Bring sticks when you arrive at the bridge—impossible to find any on site, too many visitors have collected them. Pooh Car Park at Chuck Hatch is closest to the bridge.

map_keywords: Forest Way cycling trail East Sussex

visits.text: The ruins of Brambletye House, 1631, abandoned in 1683 when its occupant fled to Spain accused of treason: ivy-covered sandstone walls in the middle of a field, a stone's throw from the village. Only a single entrance arch still stands. Free access from the public path north of Forest Row.

The smell of organic bread at the Saturday Market on Forest Row High Street—the town has become a hub for biodynamic farming and the Tablehurst community farm. Since the 1980s Forest Row has attracted an alternative community gravitating around Michael Hall School, the UK's largest Steiner school.

activities.text: Take the Forest Way, a disused railway line converted into a cycle and walking path: 15 km from Forest Row to East Grinstead, flat and shaded. The route runs alongside Weald meadows and passes Weir Wood Reservoir, a good spot for birdwatching.

practical_info.tip: The Forest Way is accessible on foot or by bike from the village. Bike hire in East Grinstead for the round trip.

visits.text: Eric Gill founded his guild of craftsmen here in 1921—the Guild of Saint Joseph and Saint Dominic—blending Catholic faith, typography and medieval arts. Edward Johnston, who designed the London Underground typeface, worked in this village. The Ditchling Museum of Art + Craft, housed in an eighteenth-century barn, preserves Gill's workshop and printing press.

Ditchling Museum, tiny and dense, is worth every minute: only two rooms, but the works of Gill, Johnston, David Jones and Ethel Mairet are gathered here with care. Gill's printing press stands in the first room. Closed Mondays.

activities.text: Climb to Ditchling Beacon for the view over the Weald: the highest point in East Sussex at 248 metres, accessible by a steep 1.5 km path from the village. On clear days, you can see to the Channel in the south and the Kent hills to the east. On the summit, traces of an Iron Age fort.

practical_info.tip: The climb from Ditchling is steep but short (20 min). Proper footwear. Small car park at the summit but often full in summer.

map_keywords: Cuckfield Holy Trinity Church West Sussex

visits.text: Moss-covered Horsham stone roofs, Cuckfield: this Weald town has preserved its fifteenth-century timber-framed façades on a High Street that turns and climbs. Around 1820, about fifty stagecoaches per day passed here on the London–Brighton road. Since the railway arrived in 1841, calm has returned.

Holy Trinity Church dates from the thirteenth century on twelfth-century foundations: its collection of Tudor and Stuart memorials makes it one of the richest in West Sussex. The monument to Charles Sergison (died 1732), commissioner of the Royal Navy, shows Truth seated on a sarcophagus, holding a mirror. Not often seen in a village church.

activities.text: Stroll the medieval High Street then head to Borde Hill Garden two kilometres to the north: over 200 acres of botanical parkland, collections of rhododendrons and rare trees, views over the Weald. Open April to October.

practical_info.tip: Borde Hill Garden is paid entry (around £10 adults). Accessible on foot from Cuckfield if you like walking—about 30 min across the fields.

visits.text: If you arrive at dawn in winter, thousands of wigeon cover the flooded meadows at Pulborough Brooks: a sound of continuous whistles impossible to describe otherwise. The RSPB reserve of 256 hectares in the Arun valley is designated a Site of Special Scientific Interest and Ramsar site. In summer, nightingales sing until June.

Pulborough Brooks, 256 hectares of wetland in the Arun Valley of the South Downs National Park: flood meadows, reedbeds, heaths and woods. The reserve hosts wintering duck and wader species, spring lapwings and nightingales in display, summer dragonflies. Views over the South Downs from the hides.

activities.text: Settle into one of the hides before 8 a.m. for the best observations. RSPB volunteers are on hand during the day to identify species. The Wetland Trail is closed to dogs—the woodland trail is open to them on leads.

practical_info.tip: Café open 9.30 a.m.–4.30 p.m. Free parking. The reserve is 3 km from Pulborough village, signposted from the A283 towards Storrington.

visits.text: A chalk quarry in the middle of the South Downs: hard to imagine today, but this site was an active industry from 1840 to the 1960s. Amberley Museum spreads over 36 acres in the old quarries, with the De Witt lime kilns from 1905, a narrow-gauge locomotive and more than 40 exhibits on south-east England's industry.

The lime kilns at Amberley Museum still exhale a mineral smell that recalls the industrial activity of this corner of Sussex. The Pepper family ran the quarries from 1876 until closure in the 1960s. The original buildings—weighbridge office, bag shed, locomotive shed—are still standing.

activities.text: Take the narrow-gauge train around the site: a period locomotive traces a circuit of a few hundred metres through the quarries. Included in the entry price, as is the vintage bus. On-site craftsmen—blacksmith, potter, wood turner—work on certain specific days. Check the programme at amberleymuseum.co.uk.

practical_info.tip: Adult entry around £14–16. Open March–October. Amberley station is a few metres from the museum—journey from Pulborough in 5 minutes.

visits.text: Cowdray House—locals just say 'the ruins' as if everyone knows, and everyone does. One of Tudor England's great houses, burned accidentally in 1793 when carpenters forgot to properly extinguish their fire. Henry VIII and Elizabeth I both stayed here. The kitchen tower is the only intact volume. Site managed by the Cowdray Heritage Trust.

Midhurst High Street: too quiet for a town that welcomed Henry VIII on three occasions. Sixteenth- and seventeenth-century timber façades frame a still-functioning market street. The Spread Eagle, an inn on this site since the fifteenth century according to some local sources, is a good stop.

Cowdray House, begun around 1520 by Sir David Owen, presumed half-uncle of Henry VII, in the Cowdray park which still covers several thousand acres. Construction was continued by William FitzWilliam, who in 1533 obtained a royal licence to 'empark' the estate. The Grade I listed ruins were restored and reopened in 2007.

activities.text: Walk along the Rother through Cowdray Park after visiting the ruins: the park is crossed by public footpaths and the ruins reflect in the river from certain angles. Polo is played here in summer—matches open to the public on some weekends.

practical_info.tip: Cowdray ruins are not open for free access—entry via guided tours and scheduled events. Check cowdray.co.uk before arriving.

activities.text: If you have time, the South Downs Way passes two kilometres south of Midhurst: views over Cowdray park, the heaths and the Sussex plain. An hour's walk return from the centre is enough to understand why this corner of West Sussex attracts walkers.

practical_info.tip: Good connections from Midhurst to Chichester (30 min) or Guildford (1h) for public transport. Gatwick around 1h by car.

seo.h1_title: Kent and Sussex in 12 Days: The Weald, Coast and Cinque Ports
seo.intro_paragraph: This 12-day route links Whitstable and its millennial oysters to Midhurst and its Tudor ruins, sweeping across the full arc of the Weald. Moated castles, Cinque Ports harbours, literary houses and nature reserves follow one another along secondary roads through Kent, East Sussex and West Sussex.
seo.seo_hook: Kent, East Sussex and West Sussex in 12 days: Cinque Ports, Bodiam Castle, Sissinghurst, Battle Abbey and the Weald from village to village.
featured_list: Whitstable: native oysters cultivated since Roman times, port built for England's first passenger railway line
Deal Castle: Tudor rose fort built by Henry VIII in 1539, more than 140 cannon positions
Sissinghurst: Vita Sackville-West's gardens, nine acres of 'outdoor rooms' in the Weald
Rye: cobbled Mermaid Street, thirteenth-century Ypres Tower, former island town of the Cinque Ports
Bodiam Castle: moated castle from 1385, almost intact exterior reflected in the water
Battle Abbey: battlefield of 14 October 1066, slab marking Harold's death
Midhurst: Tudor ruins of Cowdray House, burned in 1793

places_taglines.GB::whitstable: Kent oyster port, railway history and sailing barge to the sea forts.
places_taglines.GB::deal: Henry VIII's Tudor rose fort facing the Dover Strait, Georgian promenade.
places_taglines.GB::folkestone: Regenerated Creative Quarter, Victorian Harbour Arm and clifftop Leas Promenade.
places_taglines.GB::tenterden: Cinque Port landlocked sixteen kilometres from the sea, Georgian High Street and steam railway.
places_taglines.GB::cranbrook: Tallest white smock mill in England, medieval wool town in the Weald.
places_taglines.GB::sissinghurst: Vita Sackville-West's gardens around an Elizabethan tower, nine acres of garden rooms.
places_taglines.GB::rye: Medieval hilltop town, cobbled Mermaid Street, Ypres Tower and former island.
places_taglines.GB::winchelsea: Town planned by Edward I in 1281, medieval cellars and church truncated by raids.
places_taglines.GB::bodiam: Moated castle from 1385, intact exterior, exterior shot for Monty Python and the Holy Grail.
places_taglines.GB::hastings: Black Stade fishermen's sheds, Britain's largest beach-launched fleet.
places_taglines.GB::battle: Exact site of the Battle of Hastings on 14 October 1066, ruined abbey.
places_taglines.GB::burwash: Bateman's, Rudyard Kipling's Jacobean house from 1902 to 1936, water mill included.
places_taglines.GB::mayfield: 40 listed buildings on a single street, former palace of the Archbishops of Canterbury.
places_taglines.GB::hartfield: Poohsticks Bridge in Ashdown Forest, original setting of Winnie the Pooh.
places_taglines.GB::forest-row: Brambletye ruins 1631, Forest Way cycle path, Weald biodynamic community.
places_taglines.GB::ditchling: Eric Gill's craftsmen village, arts and crafts museum, Ditchling Beacon at 248 m.
places_taglines.GB::cuckfield: Medieval town on the London–Brighton road, fifty stagecoaches per day in 1820.
places_taglines.GB::pulborough: 256-hectare RSPB reserve in the Arun Valley, wintering duck and spring nightingales.
places_taglines.GB::amberley: Open-air museum in a former chalk quarry, narrow-gauge railway and lime kilns.
places_taglines.GB::midhurst: Tudor ruins of Cowdray House burned in 1793, polo park on the Rother.

seo_keywords: Kent East Sussex West Sussex heritage tour

highlights: Whitstable: native oysters cultivated since Roman times
Deal Castle: Henry VIII's artillery fort built in 1539–1540
Sissinghurst: Vita Sackville-West's garden managed by the National Trust
Rye: medieval town with thirteenth-century Ypres Tower and cobbled Mermaid Street
Bodiam Castle: moated castle from 1385, perfect reflection in the water
Battle Abbey: site of the Battle of Hastings, 14 October 1066
Bateman's: Rudyard Kipling's house in Burwash, built in 1634

itineraries.title: Surrey to Kent: Castles and Gardens in 10 Days
itineraries.subtitle: From Guildford to Canterbury's doorstep—10 days through the Weald between the Surrey Hills and the Kent lowlands.
itineraries.notes: Sissinghurst is closed Wednesdays and Thursdays—check nationaltrust.org.uk. Sackville College only opens Friday-Saturday from mid-June to mid-September. Chilham Castle Gardens: Tuesdays and Thursdays from May to September only.

visits.text: Paved with golden sandstone and steeply descending toward the Wey, Guildford's High Street follows the exact Saxon route from the year 900—the town received its royal charter from Henry III in 1257. The famous projecting Guildhall clock from 1683 has hung over the street for three centuries. The ensemble is regularly used as a filming location for its visual coherence. Allow 45 minutes to walk down and back up.

Guildford Castle's Norman keep is modest, but it's the only royal castle ever built in Surrey. Erected after the Conquest of 1066, it was sold to the Guildford Corporation in 1885. The garden surrounding the keep is one of the town's most floriferous. Climb to the top for a view across the Surrey Hills on the horizon. Entry ticket around £5 (check on site).

The scent of polished wood and old ceramics at Guildford Museum heralds a richer collection than expected: the medieval merchant town, Lewis Carroll who stayed here regularly in his final years, the Arts & Crafts movement in Surrey. The life-size statue of Alice in Wonderland in the Castle Grounds commemorates Carroll, who lived with his sister in Guildford and died here in 1898. Free entry.

activities.text: Walk up the High Street to the Guildhall with its clock jutting over the road, then continue to Quarry Street to see the castle ruins and the Tunsgate Arch. Guildford offers guided walking tours departing from the Museum—ideal for catching the medieval lanes running perpendicular to High Street.

practical_info.tip: Guided tours available via visitsurrey.com; otherwise the downloadable map from the website suffices for a self-guided stroll.

activities.text: If you're passing through on a Friday or Saturday morning, Guildford's market sets up on High Street and North Street with local producers from the Surrey Hills. Cheeses, artisan breads, seasonal vegetables. Perfect for assembling a picnic for Shere the next day.

practical_info.tip: The monthly Farmers' Market (usually the first Friday of the month) is the best stocked; check dates on guildford.gov.uk.

visits.text: Film set and real village: Shere plays both roles effortlessly. The Holiday was shot here in 2006 with artificial snow—the village is already so photogenic that producers dared not alter it. The Tillingbourne flows under a low stone bridge through the main street, ducks linger there, and the 16th-17th century half-timbered houses lean slightly toward it. Fewer than 1,000 residents, no traffic lights, a handful of shops.

Mentioned in the Domesday Book, Shere's St James's Church dates in its current form from the 12th century. Inside is a tiny cell walled up in which Christine Carpenter, a religious anchoress, voluntarily enclosed herself in the 14th century. The window through which food was passed to her is still visible. Free entry, generally open during the day.

activities.text: Walk from the village toward Newlands Corner, a viewpoint about 3 km into the Surrey Hills where on clear days the vista extends to the South Downs. It's near here that Agatha Christie's car was found in 1926 when the novelist staged her own disappearance. Well-marked path, roughly 90 minutes return.

practical_info.tip: Wear waterproof shoes in autumn and winter—paths in the Surrey Hills are often muddy.

visits.text: The view from the summit of Box Hill drops suddenly onto the Weald, 172 metres below, with the meanders of the Mole on the right. The white chalk slopes host wild orchids in spring and some thirty species of butterfly in summer. The zigzag road on the north face was already a promenade spot in the 17th century—and the 2012 Olympic cycling circuit passed through. National Trust property, paid parking at the top.

Dorking locals call the zigzag path 'the Zig-Zag'—it's mainly where they gather to eat ice cream after a walk. The eccentric Major Peter Labilliere, who died in 1800, asked to be buried upside down at the top of Box Hill, reckoning that the world was so upside down that he wanted 'to be the right way up at the end'. His grave is still there.

activities.text: Denbies Wine Estate, England's largest single-estate vineyard with its south-facing chalk hills, offers guided tours with tastings. Commercial exploitation on this Dorking site dates from the 1980s. Booking recommended for tours during summer.

practical_info.tip: The scenic vineyard train (Vineyard Train Tour) is the most popular format; available April to October weather permitting.

visits.text: East Grinstead's medieval High Street is stranger than it appears: it claims one of the longest continuous runs of 14th-century timber-framed houses in England, yet the town was also the site of pioneering reconstructive surgery—the Queen Victoria Hospital treated RAF pilots burned during the Battle of Britain here. A plaque on the south side of the street commemorates three Protestants burned at the stake in 1556. It all coexists along 300 metres of pavement.

Sackville College, founded by the will of Robert Sackville 2nd Earl of Dorset in 1609 and built in the following years, conceals behind its local sandstone facade a Great Hall with original hammerbeam roof and minstrels' gallery. The Reverend John Mason Neale, warden of the college from 1846 to 1866, composed Good King Wenceslas here—his study is preserved intact. Guided tour only, Friday-Saturday from mid-June to mid-September (£7.50).

Linseed oil and old oak welcome you at East Grinstead Museum with a richer story than anticipated: Weald iron works, the plastic surgeons of Queen Victoria Hospital, and the 'Guinea Pig Club'—the circle of pilots reconstructed by Sir Archibald McIndoe. Free entry; his statue stands outside Sackville College.

activities.text: Take the steam train on the Bluebell Railway from East Grinstead Station for a return trip to Sheffield Park—11 miles through Sussex on the UK's first preserved standard-gauge heritage railway, reopened in 1960 less than three years after the line closed. Allow 3 hours for the round trip with a stop at Sheffield Park.

practical_info.tip: Book online for summer weekends; the Golden Arrow Pullman dining train requires weeks of advance booking.

activities.text: If you're visiting on a Thursday morning, East Grinstead's farmers' market sets up on High Street—the market charter dates from 1247. Sussex cheeses, artisan breads, Weald producers. Perfect for stocking up before the drive to Hever.

practical_info.tip: The market runs Thursday mornings; arrive before 11am for the best selection.

visits.text: Arts and Crafts preached simplicity and craftsmanship against industry, then built houses like Standen—Grade I listed, designed by Philip Webb between 1891 and 1894, with William Morris tapestries in every room and the original electric light fittings still in service. Webb integrated the barns of an earlier farm into his new construction, creating the impression of a place that had always been there. The paradox is delightful. Timed entry tickets on weekdays.

activities.text: Explore the paths of the Standen estate that link the house to Kingscote station on the Bluebell Railway—a two-hour woodland loop through ancient Weald woods with semi-wild Exmoor ponies. Map available for free download from the National Trust website before departure.

practical_info.tip: Waterproof shoes recommended even in summer on the estate paths.

visits.text: Hever Castle dates to 1383 in its original form, enlarged by the Boleyns after their acquisition in 1462—this is where Anne Boleyn grew up before becoming Henry VIII's second wife, for exactly 1,000 days. The double-moated castle greets you with a drawbridge and two portcullises in sequence. Inside: period Tudor portraits, two prayer books that belonged to Anne, and a collection reputed among the finest of Tudor portraits after the National Portrait Gallery.

Hever's interior is not authentic—William Waldorf Astor, the fabulously wealthy American owner, redid the whole thing in the early 20th century with period tapestries and furniture brought from Europe. The result is meticulous and the distinction between original and restoration well documented in the included audio guide. What is authentic, however: the moats, the drawbridge, and the 14th-century gatehouse.

activities.text: Climbing roses, shimmering moats, pleached lime trees—Hever's gardens spread over 125 acres with an Italian Garden of columns brought from Italy by Astor, a plant maze, and an artificial lake dug in 1904. Allow at least two hours for the garden alone. The rhododendrons in May are particularly spectacular.

practical_info.tip: The quietest day is usually Tuesday outside school holidays; avoid May weekends.

visits.text: Hard to believe an entire village can belong to a single organization—yet the National Trust bought all of Chiddingstone in 1939 to freeze it in its Tudor state. Houses, inn, and shop have been preserved ever since. Over two-thirds of the buildings are more than two hundred years old. The village shop is one of the oldest houses on the street. There's only one street; you can walk it in ten minutes.

Denys Eyre Bower had a problem: he couldn't stop collecting. When he moved into Chiddingstone Castle in 1955, he filled this neo-Gothic manor with a Tudor core (remodeled in the early 19th century by architect Henry Kendall) with samurai armor, ancient Egyptian treasures, Stuart paintings, and Jacobite manuscripts. Everything has remained intact since his death. Open April to October.

activities.text: Local beer, shaded garden, Tudor houses opposite—The Castle Inn at Chiddingstone has been serving travelers since the 15th century. Lunch on the terrace overlooking the village's only street. No bookings for outside tables; check opening hours midweek off-season.

practical_info.tip: The pub may close Mondays off-season; check their website beforehand.

visits.text: Penshurst Place was granted in 1552 by Edward VI to Sir William Sidney, his tutor and steward—the castle had previously served as a hunting lodge for Henry VIII during his courtship of Anne Boleyn (Hever Castle is six kilometers away). The Sidney family still owns it, making it one of England's oldest family homes. The 14th-century Baron's Hall is one of the rare examples of a medieval domestic great hall still intact.

The Sidneys extinguished their male line in the 18th century but their heirs took up the name—today the De L'Isles—and continue to manage Penshurst. Queen Elizabeth I stayed here on several occasions and the Queen Elizabeth Room bears her name. There's also a Toy Museum with toys from successive generations of Sidneys, including a doll's house and 19th-century lead soldiers.

activities.text: Explore the 11 acres of walled gardens at Penshurst, divided into 'rooms' by more than a mile of yew hedges—a structure resembling what Vita Sackville-West would create at Sissinghurst a generation later. The rose beds in June and tulips in April-May are highlights. The heritage apple orchard is also worth a detour in autumn.

practical_info.tip: Open April to November; closed Mondays and Tuesdays outside holidays. Check penshurstplace.com.

visits.text: Dudley Lord North fell ill in 1606, drank from a rusty-red iron spring in the Weald, felt better, and accidentally triggered the creation of an entire town. The Pantiles, a Georgian colonnade built through the 18th century when royalty and aristocracy came to take the waters, remains the heart of the spa. The chalybeate spring is still there at the north end of the promenade. The town received the title 'Royal' in 1909 from Edward VII.

Walk up to Calverley Park, the work of architect Decimus Burton in the 1820s—the same Burton who worked on Hyde Park Corner in London. Neoclassical villas around a preserved public park, far from the shopping center. The adjacent Calverley Grounds remain freely accessible.

The iron flavor of the chalybeate spring at the Pantiles is served by costumed attendants—the water is lukewarm and metallic, which isn't unpleasant if you're expecting it. The Amelia Scott, a museum and library merged and opened in 2022, exhibits the spa town's history with original objects including a period pantile. Free, open 7 days a week except public holidays.

activities.text: Chic but unpretentious: the Pantiles shops mix independent art galleries, antique dealers, and terrace cafes. On Thursday evenings in summer, a free Jazz Festival sets up there. A craft and farmers' market runs fortnightly. Allow time to browse rather than buy.

practical_info.tip: The Jazz Festival runs Thursday evenings in summer; dates on visittunbridgewells.com.

activities.text: The Amelia Scott deserves better than its reputation as a merged library-museum: the 'Story of the Wells' exhibition traces the spa's origins with original objects. The Fine Grind cafe on the ground floor overlooks a sculpture garden with works commissioned for the building. Allow 45 minutes.

practical_info.tip: Closed on public holidays; check theamelia.co.uk beforehand.

visits.text: Built after 1066 to control the Medway crossing on the route from Hastings to London, Tonbridge Castle is the work of Richard FitzGilbert, cousin of William the Conqueror. The 13th-century golden sandstone gatehouse is one of the finest examples of its kind in England. The included audio guide covers nine hundred years of history from the first timber motte-and-bailey.

If the monthly market is on that day—usually the second Saturday of the month—settle by the Medway with Kent cheeses and artisan bread. The redeveloped riverfront facing the castle offers tables with views of the medieval gatehouse. On weekdays, the artisan bakers on High Street do just as well.

activities.text: Embark with Tonbridge River Trips aboard the Enid May, an electric cruiser with the skipper's commentary on the town's medieval history. Kingfishers, cormorants, and dragonflies animate the banks between Georgian houses and the first reaches of the nature park. Available in summer; durations from 45 minutes to 2 hours depending on the chosen excursion.

practical_info.tip: Rowing boats are also available for hire by Big Bridge.

visits.text: The castle floats. Set on two islands in a natural lake amid 500 acres of Kent parkland, Leeds Castle is one of England's most photographed medieval sites. The first stone fortification dates to 1119; Edward I turned it into a royal palace in 1278 for Queen Eleanor of Castile. Six queens of England lived here. Lady Baillie, the last private owner, bequeathed everything to a foundation on her death in 1974.

You'd expect an armory, you find a dog collar museum in the old stables. The collection—the world's largest of its type—assembles over 130 pieces from the 16th to the 19th century, donated to the foundation in 1977 by Gertrude Hunt. The earliest iron-spiked examples protected hunting dogs' throats against wolves and bears that ran through England's forests. Included in the ticket.

A dog collar museum in a medieval castle, and also: a birds of prey aviary, black swans on the lake, daily falconry, the Gloriette—a 13th-century keep with reconstructed royal apartments. Henry VIII undertook major works here in the 16th century. The multimedia audio guide included in the ticket covers nine centuries of history.

activities.text: Get lost in the maze of 2,400 yews planted in 1988—designed to truly lose you: the classic 'hand on the wall' rule doesn't work here, the walls aren't all connected. At the center, an underground grotto with mythological deities carved into the walls. Allow 30 to 45 minutes.

practical_info.tip: The maze may close in high winds; included in the castle ticket.

activities.text: Attend Leeds Castle's daily falconry—buzzards, hawks, and eagles in free flight over the park with commentary from the falconers. The estate also houses an exotic bird aviary and the famous black swans on the lake. Displays usually run twice daily depending on season; times posted at the entrance.

practical_info.tip: Check falconry times at the estate entrance; included in the day ticket.

visits.text: In 1930, Vita Sackville-West paid £12,375 for these ruins—no running water, no electricity, no heating. She saw in them a substitute for Knole, the ancestral house from which she'd been excluded from inheriting. What she made of it in thirty years remains one of England's most influential gardens: seven walled plant 'rooms', each with its theme. The geometric structure came from her husband Harold Nicolson; the romantic chaos of the plantings, from her.

White, silver-grey, pale green—the White Garden explodes in June with its white roses, silvery foliage, and clematis climbing the central framework. The mulliganii rose cascading over the central arbor in mid-June has been photographed for decades. Arrive before 10am on a Friday or Monday to visit without crowds.

The Elizabethan tower is small, the 78 wooden stairs steep and worn—but the view from the top over the seven gardens and the 450-acre estate is worth the effort. Vita's study on the first floor is preserved as she left it. This is where she wrote her garden columns for the Observer until her death in 1962. Queue in high season.

activities.text: Explore the 450 acres of farmland beyond the formal gardens—a working farm with marked paths through Weald woods and meadows. The main loop takes 90 minutes and passes the original barns and oast houses. Maps at reception. The estate is accessible even without a ticket for the formal garden.

practical_info.tip: The National Trust second-hand bookshop on site has a remarkable 'gardens and botany' section.

activities.text: In June, come early: the Rose Garden reaches its peak in the third week of the month, when Vita's old roses are in simultaneous bloom. She preferred heritage roses to modern hybrids—an almost militant stance for the time. Sissinghurst closes Wednesdays and Thursdays.

practical_info.tip: Closed Wednesdays and Thursdays; check nationaltrust.org.uk before planning.

visits.text: Faversham's Abbey Street is one of England's finest medieval streets: 15th-16th century timber-framed houses, exposed beams, vaulted passages—all in a state of preservation owing as much to the town's geographic isolation as to its heritage protections. Faversham has over 500 listed buildings. The Fleur de Lis Heritage Centre, at the entrance, traces the town's history from prehistory.

Shepherd Neame, officially founded in 1698, has been brewing on its Court Street site since at least 1573 according to local records. It's Britain's oldest brewery in continuous commercial operation. Guided tours show the copper vessels, the brewhouse, and the collection of period delivery vehicles. The tasting at the end is included.

Locals simply call the brewery 'Shep's'—and the sweet scent of boiling hops sometimes fills all of Court Street on brewing days. Faversham was also one of the centers of the gunpowder industry; Chart Mills, the powder mills on the creek north of town, is the world's oldest gunpowder works still standing. Free access to the outdoor site.

activities.text: Wander Faversham's medieval market—three times a week (Tuesday, Friday, Saturday) in the market square surrounded by Tudor and Georgian houses. It's Kent's oldest market. The Creek east of town, a former seaport, can be walked along a towpath with views of restored barges.

practical_info.tip: Saturday market is the best stocked; Macknade Fine Foods, at the town entrance, offers a remarkable regional food shop.

activities.text: Useful contradiction: Chart Mills, the world's oldest gunpowder works still standing, sits in a bird nature reserve. The Oare Gunpowder Works site supplied powder for Trafalgar and Waterloo. Today 81 acres of marshland with snipe, plovers, and ducks. Wheelchair-accessible paths, free entry.

practical_info.tip: The Oare marshes are 2 km north of town; map on kentwildlifetrust.org.uk.

visits.text: Chilham's square smells of old oak and damp stone after rain. Surrounded on three sides by 17th-century Jacobean timber-framed houses, it opens on the fourth side toward the 13th-century St Mary's Church and the castle gates. The ensemble forms one of the region's most photographed architectural sets. The White Horse Inn dates from the 15th century. The village is 7 miles from Canterbury.

Chilham's Norman keep dates to 1174 and is still inhabited, making it one of Britain's oldest residential buildings. The adjacent Jacobean manor was built in 1616 by Sir Dudley Digges, close associate of Shakespeare and merchant adventurer. The gardens, whose design is attributed to John Tradescant the Elder, are open Tuesdays and Thursdays from May to September.

activities.text: If you arrive on a Tuesday or Thursday between May and September, Chilham Castle gardens open from 10am to 4pm (£6, cash only). Three terraces descend to a lake with views over the Stour valley. Outside these hours, the village square, church, and marked paths around the estate are freely accessible.

practical_info.tip: Cash only at the castle gardens ticket desk; no credit cards accepted.

seo.h1_title: Surrey to Kent: Castles and Gardens in 10 Days
seo.intro_paragraph: This 10-day circuit traverses the Weald from west to east, from Guildford and the Surrey Hills to Canterbury's doorstep. It passes through the film-set village of Shere, the Surrey Hills at Box Hill, East Grinstead's Jacobean almshouse, Hever Castle where Anne Boleyn grew up, the Pantiles of Tunbridge Wells, Leeds Castle on its lake, Sissinghurst's gardens, and the medieval brewing town of Faversham.
seo.seo_hook: Tudor castles, Vita Sackville-West's legendary gardens, and Britain's oldest brewery: 10 days through the Weald between the Surrey Hills and Canterbury's gates.
featured_list: Hever Castle: Anne Boleyn's childhood home, 14th-century double moat, 125 acres of gardens
Sissinghurst: Vita Sackville-West's 'room' gardens, bought as ruins for £12,375 in 1930
Leeds Castle: royal castle on two islands and the world's only dog collar museum
Faversham: Shepherd Neame brewery founded 1698, Britain's oldest
Chilham: Jacobean square and Norman keep from 1174 still inhabited

places_taglines.GB::guildford: County town of Surrey on a Saxon route, with Norman castle and Georgian High Street.
places_taglines.GB::shere: Timber-framed Surrey Hills village, filming location for The Holiday, 12th-century church.
places_taglines.GB::dorking: Surrey market town at the foot of Box Hill, home to England's largest single-estate vineyard.
places_taglines.GB::east-grinstead: Medieval town pioneering reconstructive surgery, with Sackville College Jacobean almshouse from 1609.
places_taglines.GB::hever: 14th-century double-moated castle, childhood home of Anne Boleyn, 125 acres of gardens.
places_taglines.GB::chiddingstone: One-street Tudor village, entirely preserved by the National Trust since 1939.
places_taglines.GB::penshurst: Medieval manor of the Sidneys since 1552, 14th-century Baron's Hall and 11 acres of walled gardens.
places_taglines.GB::royal-tunbridge-wells: Royal spa town born in 1606 with Georgian colonnade of the Pantiles and chalybeate spring.
places_taglines.GB::tonbridge: Medieval market on the Medway with 13th-century Norman gatehouse.
places_taglines.GB::leeds-castle: 12th-century royal castle on two islands, the world's only dog collar museum.
places_taglines.GB::sissinghurst: Vita Sackville-West's 'room' gardens, bought as ruins in 1930, bequeathed to the National Trust in 1962.
places_taglines.GB::faversham: Medieval town with 500+ listed buildings and Shepherd Neame, Britain's brewery founded in 1698.
places_taglines.GB::chilham: Jacobean village around a picture-postcard square, Norman keep from 1174 still inhabited.

highlights: Hever Castle: 14th-century moated castle, childhood home of Anne Boleyn
Sissinghurst: Vita Sackville-West's 'room' gardens, bought as ruins in 1930 for £12,375
Leeds Castle: royal castle on two islands and the world's only dog collar museum
Shepherd Neame in Faversham: British brewery founded in 1698, the oldest in the country
